div.div: · 5 geometric pieces that form a cross

Cut out the five puzzle pieces that together form a Greek cross shape. Your challenge: rearrange the same five pieces into a perfect square with no gaps or overlaps. Some pieces may need to be rotated or flipped. The surprising mathematical fact: a cross and a square of equal area can always be rearranged into each other using just five pieces. This is known as a dissection puzzle — the pieces are hinged by geometry, not glue. Print a template from any puzzle website to cut your own pieces.

Henry Dudeney published his elegant 4-piece cross-to-square dissection in 1902, improving on earlier 5-piece solutions. The pieces can even be hinged together to fold from one shape to the other — a 'hinged dissection' that Dudeney demonstrated in silver at the Royal Institution.

A Greek cross is cut into five pieces. The puzzle is to rearrange these five pieces to form a perfect square. The solution requires a specific rotation and arrangement that is far from obvious — the cross-shaped whole must become a rectangular whole.

svg.text: Source: Puzzles Old and New (1893) · Heritage Parlor
